fr:mainmatter.html:p: The type of isomorphisms onto C is inhabited, considering the identity isomorphism 1_{C}\colon C\cong C. Therefore, it suffices to show that the type of isomorphisms onto C depicted below is a proposition:
We unfold the definitions of a precategories and their isomorphisms, dropping propositional axioms:
A function that is both propositional and a surjection, i.e. a bijection, is an equivalence by Corollary . We can therefore repackage our telescope equivalently:
Next we rewrite using the univalence principles for functions and families of types (Axiom , Axiom ):
By the univalence principle for functions, the data above is contractible.
A precategory C is specified by the following data:

html:ol.html:li: for each x\in C, an identity arrow 1_{x}\colon x\to x;
for each f\colon x\to y and g\colon y\to z, a composite arrow g\circ f\colon x \to z;

such that composition is associative and unital with respect to identity arrows:
\boldsymbol {\lambda }_{f}\colon 1_{y}\circ f = f\quad \boldsymbol {\rho }_{f}\colon f\circ 1_{x} = f \quad \boldsymbol {\alpha }_{f,g,h}\colon \mathopen {}\left \lparen h\circ g\right \rparen \mathclose {} \circ f = h\circ \mathopen {}\left \lparen g\circ f\right \rparen \mathclose {}

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A functor F\colon C\to D from a precategory C to a precategory D is defined by the following data:

html:ol.html:li:
such that the action on morphisms commutes with identities and compositions:
F^{1_{x}}\colon F1_{x} = 1_{Fx}\quad F^{g\circ f}\colon F \mathopen {}\left \lparen g\circ f\right \rparen \mathclose {} = Fg\circ Ff

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A functor F\colon C \to D is called (full, faithful) when the action on arrows is valued in (surjections, embeddings) respectively. It is common to refer to a full and faithful functor as fully faithful.
Fullness and faithfulness are both properties of a functor.
A functor F\colon C \to D is called an isomorphism, written f\colon C\cong D, when F is fully faithful and its action on objects is an equivalence of types.
Being an isomorphism is a proposition.

The type of isomorphisms onto C is inhabited, considering the identity isomorphism 1_{C}\colon C\cong C. Therefore, it suffices to show that the type of isomorphisms onto C depicted below is a proposition:
We unfold the definitions of a precategories and their isomorphisms, dropping propositional axioms:
A function that is both propositional and a surjection, i.e. a bijection, is an equivalence by Corollary . We can therefore repackage our telescope equivalently:
Next we rewrite using the univalence principles for functions and families of types (Axiom , Axiom ):
By the univalence principle for functions, the data above is contractible.

A type A is said to be contractible with centre a:A when every x:A is equipped with an identification \chi _x\colon a = x. We say that A is contractible when it comes equipped with some a:A such that A is contractible with centre a. We sometimes refer to a contractible type as a singleton.
A functor F\colon C \to D is called an isomorphism, written f\colon C\cong D, when F is fully faithful and its action on objects is an equivalence of types.
Being an isomorphism is a proposition.
A precategory C is specified by the following data:

html:ol.html:li: for each x\in C, an identity arrow 1_{x}\colon x\to x;
for each f\colon x\to y and g\colon y\to z, a composite arrow g\circ f\colon x \to z;

such that composition is associative and unital with respect to identity arrows:
\boldsymbol {\lambda }_{f}\colon 1_{y}\circ f = f\quad \boldsymbol {\rho }_{f}\colon f\circ 1_{x} = f \quad \boldsymbol {\alpha }_{f,g,h}\colon \mathopen {}\left \lparen h\circ g\right \rparen \mathclose {} \circ f = h\circ \mathopen {}\left \lparen g\circ f\right \rparen \mathclose {}
